text: The surgeon displays a jejunostomy tube (J-tube) with a small balloon, preparing to insert it laparoscopically through a small incision.
This is a catheter guide for urology. What we do is we stick this through it, and we use this as a guide to get this in. And we get it to about right here.
The surgeon positions the J-tube, preparing to cut it with scissors to increase rigidity, facilitating its insertion into the small intestine.

A laparoscopic J-tube is really just a pain in the butt, but in some patients that have had previous stomach surgery or have real bad aspiration, it's the only option. They don't really make a J-tube kit. So we kind of have to make this up as we go.
The surgeon measures approximately fifteen centimeters from the area of concern, cuts the tube with scissors, and inserts it into the designated site within the small intestine.
The surgeon identifies the proximal and distal segments of the jejunum, applying three small burns on the distal surface and one on the proximal surface. A hole is then made in the jejunum.

The surgeon inserts the jejunostomy tube, equipped with a catheter guide, into the small intestine through the previously created hole. The catheter guide provides rigidity, facilitating the smooth insertion of the tube into the intestinal lumen.
The surgeon inflates the balloon at the balloon port, securing the small intestine to the abdominal wall and ensuring the jejunostomy tube is positioned distally.

The surgeon inflates the balloon, which is now larger than the hole in the small intestine. The next step involves sewing the hole closed around the jejunostomy tube using a purse string stitch, followed by imbricating the tissue around the tube.
The surgeon positions the jejunostomy tube up to the abdominal wall, completing the placement of the laparoscopic J-tube, which is visibly secured and ready for use.
